Controlling Page Breaks

Controlling Page Breaks

A page break is the point at which Uniface inserts a trailer frame (if this is in the layout), issues an eject instruction, and continues printing on the following page.

By default, Uniface inserts page breaks at the point where the page stops, as defined in the page length definitions in the print job model. This can result in blocks of information that should remain together being incorrectly split up and printed on two pages due to lack of space on a page.

You may also want to control the position of page breaks based on the data, for example, inserting a page break when the value of a specified field changes between occurrences. (In this case, the sort order of the retrieved data may be important!)

To control page breaks, you can:

  • Set the property Page feed after occurrence to protect an occurrence or frame from being split.
  • Use skip and eject to set page breaks in ProcScript. For more information, see skip and eject.
  • Test for data changes between occurrences to insert conditional page breaks.

Page Feed Property

To prevent blocks of information from being incorrectly split, set the Page feed after occurrence to one of the following values:

  • F (Print Frame on Same Page)—issues an eject before the frame if there is not enough room on the current page to print it. This is valid for entities, named area frames, and break frames. For example, you may want to print a break frame which contains too much text to fit on the remainder of your page. If the break frame is protected, Uniface issues an eject before printing it.
  • O (Print Occurrence on Same Page)—issues an eject before the occurrence if there is not enough room on the current page to print it.
Note: Uniface does not take expansion of field or entity frames into consideration with this form of protection. Use the $lines ProcScript function to protect occurrences or frames if expansion can occur.

Controlling Page Breaks in Proc

The skip and eject ProcScript statements tell the printer to skip a specified number of lines, or start a new page before printing an occurrence. Uniface automatically handles formatting header and trailer frames, if they have been defined.

You should use these statements in the following triggers:

  • trigger getFocus (Frame) (Frame)
  • trigger getFocus (Entity) (Entity)
  • trigger leavePrinted

Example: Printing Each Occurrence on a Separate Page

To print each occurrence of an outer entity on a separate page, use the eject statement in the getFocus or leavePrinted trigger of the outer entity. The trigger you use depends on whether you prefer to insert the page break at the beginning or end of each occurrence. For example:

trigger getFocus ; of entity CUSTOMER
 eject
end

If you want the first page to be empty, make the skip statement the first statement. Uniface ignores the eject statement on an empty page, so the first occurrence still appears on page one, provided that the outer entity is positioned so that the top left corner is in the first position of the first line under the header frame (if defined).

Example: Data-Driven Page Breaks

In many cases, you may want to insert a page break based on the values of the data in the report. For example, to force a page break before each occurrence where the invoice date changes, you need compare occurrences.

Note: If you are printing by date, be sure to retrieve the occurrences in date order also using the read trigger of the entity concerned.
trigger read ; of entity INVOICE
 read order by "INVDATE"
end

And:

trigger leavePrinted ; of entity INVOICE
 compare/next (INVDATE) from "INVOICE" ;compare date of next invoice
 if ($result = 0) ;if next date does not match
 eject ;force a page break
 endif
end ; leavePrinted

Alternatively, you can test for the next occurrence in the leavePrinted trigger:

trigger leavePrinted ; of entity INVOICE
if (INVDATE.INVOICE != $next(INVDATE.INVOICE))
 eject
endif
end ; leavePrinted
Related Links
  • Triggers for Printing

    Much report writing is data-driven—you might want to print a number of different things in different situations, depending on the data in the report. This involves using conditional ProcScript that checks what is happening in the form and directs the print process accordingly.

  • ProcScript for Printing and Reports

    The ProcScript language provides print-specific statements and functions, but there are also instructions that should be avoided in reports.

  • Selecting and Sorting Data for Printing

    Selecting and sorting your data before printing is critical to good reports. If it is not feasible to select and sort your data before printing, you can also manipulate occurrences during printing, although this is less efficient.

  • eject

    Eject a page when printing.

  • skip

    Skip the specified number of lines when printing.

  • compare

    Compare fields of two adjacent occurrences.

  • $lines

    Return the number of lines remaining on the current page.
